meta.title: Sunny Merge — Formalize vs Invariants
meta.description: A grounded decision page separating Sunny's good ideas that should be formalized from the hard invariants the NAAC merge lane should not bend.

props.title: Sunny Work That Should Be Formalized vs Rules We Do Not Bend
props.subtitle: A clean way to adopt useful work without importing policy drift

props.content: The right question is not whether Sunny's branch is good or bad. It is which pieces are structurally useful once expressed through current authority surfaces, and which pieces violate invariants even if they improve coverage numbers. The result of this pass is intentionally narrow: preserve ideas only after they survive authority, builder, and behavior checks.

props.label: SNAPSHOT
props.title: Current Merge Posture
props.subtitle: What survived the audit, what still needs formalization, and what remains non-negotiable.

trend.value: do not trade these for coverage

props.label: FRAME
props.title: Two Lenses
props.subtitle: One lens asks whether the idea is useful. The other asks whether the implementation is allowed.

props.title: Formalize
props.content: A good candidate already points in the right direction, but it must be re-expressed through the repo's current authority model: split phraseology sources, stopgap overlays, deterministic builders, and tests that match the checked-in truth surface.

props.title: Invariant
props.content: An invariant is not a style preference. It is a guardrail that prevents us from getting greener numbers by lying about authority, pretending degraded renders are full renders, or baking scenario-specific hacks into the runtime.

props.label: GOOD THINGS
props.title: Useful Work That Should Be Formalized
props.subtitle: These are the ideas worth keeping, but only when they are expressed through canonical files and the existing build pipeline.

rows.why: Current split phraseology already has FAA-backed sequencing rows with a real SEQUENCE slot in data/atc/phraseology/templates/intents/sequencing.json.
rows.formalize: Keep the source-first path: set the stopgap format to Number {sequence}, {callsign}, rebuild faa_graph outputs, and verify both resolver and parser sequencing assertions.

rows.why: Sunny is directionally right that the parser still has a real gap on informal ATC and non-template language.
rows.formalize: Generalize the rules, remove scenario-specific tokens like N17XX, define clear precedence, and add dedicated acceptance tests instead of importing a broad catch-all classifier wholesale.

rows.why: Replies like Roger, Radar contact, and Looking are often more faithful than label-like placeholders such as Informational or Traffic advisory.
rows.formalize: Promote them one intent at a time through cited split phraseology or explicit trusted overlays with sunset conditions, not a branch-wide unblock sweep.

rows.why: When the chosen render format actually contains a slot, requiring it prevents fake full renders and keeps dispatch metadata honest.
rows.formalize: Let required slots derive from the authoritative format via the builder, then confirm parser extraction parity before claiming the route is stable.

rows.why: Targeted assertions kept this merge lane honest without pretending unrelated resolver and parser debt belonged to the same change.
rows.formalize: Continue proving the changed slice directly, then open separate cleanup lanes for broader red baselines instead of hiding that debt inside merge claims.

props.footer: Authority anchors in this pass: sequencing source template, stopgap overlay, rebuilt readback registry, rebuilt dispatch plan.

props.content: The important pattern is **promotion by formalization**, not branch loyalty. If an idea is good, it should survive being rewritten into the repo's existing split-source and builder model. If it only works as a direct patch on generated outputs or Python heuristics, it is not ready yet.

props.label: INVARIANTS
props.title: Clear Invariants This Lane Should Not Bend
props.subtitle: These are the rules that decide whether a merge is standards-clean even when the branch claims better coverage.

rows.invariant: JSON and data authority stay primary
rows.why: Readback truth is supposed to come from registry and phraseology data, not from a Python composer deciding what sounds right.
rows.violation: modules/readback/phraseology.py plus resolver post-processing that rewrites successful template renders in Python.

rows.why: A blocked or fallback surface must remain visibly degraded so metrics do not overstate coverage.
rows.violation: Flipping blocked_surface to false and changing tests to treat per-intent fallbacks as full renders.

rows.why: If a change matters, it should be made in split phraseology or stopgap source files and then regenerated deterministically.
rows.violation: Bulk edits that only make sense as generated JSON churn or monolithic phraseology reshapes outside the current source layout.

rows.invariant: No new canonical intent without end-to-end authority
rows.why: A canonical intent must exist coherently across graph, parser, contracts, registry, and tests.
rows.violation: Trying to promote emergency_fuel as a distinct canonical runtime surface after the current intent policy explicitly collapsed it back into emergency_declaration.

rows.why: A fallback rule should generalize across traffic, not key off one demo callsign or one scenario family.
rows.violation: Fallback parser patterns that explicitly match N17XX or the ownship callsign family as a catch-all signal.

rows.invariant: Adoption stays isolated from dirty main
rows.why: A merge audit is only trustworthy if it does not mutate unrelated local work or hide conflicts inside the operator checkout.
rows.violation: Materializing Sunny work directly in the dirty main tree or mixing branch roles in one checkout.

props.footer: These invariants are what kept the pass narrow: one sequencing salvage, no synthetic phraseology import.

props.content: Coverage is not the product. Authority fidelity is the product. Better numbers only count after the implementation still tells the truth about where those numbers came from.

props.label: FLOW
props.title: How This Pass Was Resolved
props.subtitle: The merge lane tightened by subtraction, not by broad acceptance.

items.title: Isolate the lane in sunny-jp-merge
items.description: Create a dedicated worktree and matching data branch so the dirty main checkout stays pure.

items.title: Audit Sunny root and data separately
items.description: Root readback generation and parser fallback were treated independently from data-side authority outputs.

items.title: Reduce the keepable set to source-backed sequencing
items.description: Sequencing already had a real SEQUENCE slot in split phraseology, so it could be promoted cleanly through the builder.

items.title: Rebuild and verify only the touched slice
items.description: Targeted resolver and parser assertions passed while unrelated red baselines were documented rather than hidden.

items.title: Decide whether to checkpoint or do a second root pass
items.description: The next choice is either to commit the sequencing-only salvage or open a narrower parser standardization pass.

props.label: WHAT IT MEANS
props.title: What This Means Now
props.subtitle: The merge strategy is clear even if the branch is mixed.

props.content:
There are good ideas on Sunny's lane, but they do not all deserve direct adoption. The rule is simple: **formalize what survives authority, keep invariants hard, and let everything else wait.**

That is why the current pass kept sequencing, rejected Python phraseology generation, deferred a root parser fallback rewrite, and treated broader resolver and parser failures as separate debt instead of fake evidence.

If we follow this pattern consistently, Sunny can still be a strong source of ideas without becoming a source of policy drift.
